text: Hypertension affects approximately 1.28 billion adults worldwide and is the leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease and premature death. The 2020 International Society of Hypertension guidelines define hypertension as systolic blood pressure ≥140 mmHg or diastolic ≥90 mmHg on two separate occasions. Optimal blood pressure is below 120/80 mmHg. Lifestyle interventions — including sodium restriction to <5g/day, DASH diet, aerobic exercise 150 min/week, weight reduction, smoking cessation, and alcohol moderation — are first-line for all patients and can reduce systolic BP by 5–20 mmHg.
source: Global Hypertension Practice Guidelines — PMID 33268196 | Hypertension. 2020

text: Antihypertensive drug therapy should be initiated when blood pressure exceeds 140/90 mmHg in low-risk patients or 130/80 mmHg in high-risk patients (diabetes, CKD, established CVD). First-line agents include ACE inhibitors (ramipril, lisinopril), ARBs (losartan, valsartan), calcium channel blockers (amlodipine), and thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone). ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred in patients with diabetes mellitus or proteinuric CKD due to renoprotective effects. Combination therapy achieves target BP more effectively than monotherapy dose escalation.
source: Pharmacological Treatment of Hypertension — PMID 31475488 | Lancet. 2019

text: Resistant hypertension is defined as blood pressure remaining above goal despite concurrent use of three antihypertensive agents of different classes, including a diuretic, at maximally tolerated doses. Prevalence is approximately 10–15% of treated hypertensive patients. Secondary causes including primary hyperaldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, obstructive sleep apnea, and pheochromocytoma must be excluded. Spironolactone added as a fourth agent provides the greatest BP reduction in true resistant hypertension, reducing systolic BP by a mean of 11 mmHg.
source: Resistant Hypertension — PMID 29133354 | NEJM. 2017

text: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a progressive metabolic disorder characterised by hyperglycaemia resulting from insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. Global prevalence has tripled since 1980 to 537 million adults in 2021. Diagnosis requires fasting plasma glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL), 2-hour glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L on OGTT, HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%), or random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L with symptoms. Lifestyle modification — structured physical activity, caloric restriction, and 5–10% weight loss — can delay or prevent progression from prediabetes to T2DM.
source: Global Epidemiology of Type 2 Diabetes — PMID 34161073 | Lancet. 2021

text: Metformin remains the preferred initial glucose-lowering agent for most patients with type 2 diabetes due to its established efficacy (HbA1c reduction 1–1.5%), cardiovascular safety, weight neutrality, low hypoglycaemia risk, and low cost. It acts by reducing hepatic glucose production and improving peripheral insulin sensitivity via AMPK activation. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide) and SGLT-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) are preferred second-line agents in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
source: Management of Hyperglycaemia in Type 2 Diabetes — PMID 30291128 | Diabetologia. 2019

text: SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular mortality by 38%, hospitalisation for heart failure by 35%, and progression of kidney disease by 45% in patients with type 2 diabetes and established cardiovascular disease. Empagliflozin, canagliflozin, and dapagliflozin have all demonstrated cardiovascular and renal benefits in large randomised trials (EMPA-REG OUTCOME, CANVAS, DECLARE-TIMI 58). Adverse effects include urogenital infections, diabetic ketoacidosis (rare), and Fournier's gangrene (rare). They should be used with caution in patients with eGFR <45 mL/min/1.73m².
source: SGLT-2 Inhibitors Cardiovascular and Renal Benefits — PMID 33053784 | NEJM. 2020

text: Semaglutide, a GLP-1 receptor agonist, reduces HbA1c by 1.5–1.8% and body weight by 4–6 kg when used as weekly subcutaneous injection. The SUSTAIN-6 trial demonstrated a 26% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) versus placebo. Oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) provides comparable glycaemic and weight benefits. Common side effects include nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea, typically self-limiting. GLP-1 RAs are contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN-2 syndrome.
source: Semaglutide in Type 2 Diabetes — PMID 29677303 | NEJM. 2018

text: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF, LVEF <40%) affects 50% of heart failure patients and carries a 5-year mortality exceeding 50%. The four pillars of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) that reduce mortality are: ACE inhibitors/ARBs or ARNi (sacubitril/valsartan), beta-blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol), mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone), and SGLT-2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) reduces cardiovascular death and HF hospitalisation by 20% compared to enalapril.
source: 2021 ESC Heart Failure Guidelines — PMID 33215916 | Eur Heart J. 2021

text: Acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF) presenting with fluid overload requires prompt intravenous diuresis with furosemide. Initial IV furosemide dose should be at least equivalent to the patient's oral maintenance dose. SGLT-2 inhibitors accelerate decongestion and reduce 30-day rehospitalisation when initiated during ADHF admission. BNP or NT-proBNP guided therapy helps optimise diuretic dosing. Early initiation of neurohormonal antagonists (ACEi/ARB/ARNi, beta-blockers, MRA) before discharge improves post-discharge outcomes.
source: Acute Heart Failure Management — PMID 31535104 | JACC. 2019

text: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, affecting 37.5 million people globally. It increases stroke risk 5-fold. Stroke risk stratification using the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score guides anticoagulation decisions: score ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women warrants oral anticoagulation. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs — apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin due to superior efficacy, reduced intracranial bleeding, and no requirement for routine INR monitoring.
source: 2020 ESC Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines — PMID 31504429 | Eur Heart J. 2020

text: Rate control versus rhythm control strategies in atrial fibrillation have been debated for two decades. The EAST-AFNET 4 trial demonstrated that early rhythm control (within 1 year of AF diagnosis) significantly reduced cardiovascular death, stroke, and hospitalisation compared to usual care. Rhythm control options include antiarrhythmic drugs (flecainide, propafenone for structurally normal hearts; amiodarone, sotalol for structural heart disease) and catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation), which achieves superior rhythm control with 65–70% freedom from AF at 1 year.
source: Early Rhythm Control in Atrial Fibrillation — PMID 32865373 | NEJM. 2020

text: Asthma is a heterogeneous chronic airway disease affecting 358 million people worldwide, characterised by variable airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and type 2 airway inflammation. Diagnosis requires demonstration of reversible airflow obstruction: ≥12% and 200 mL improvement in FEV1 after short-acting bronchodilator, or positive bronchial challenge test. Type 2 high asthma (elevated eosinophils, FeNO ≥25 ppb, or IgE sensitisation) responds best to inhaled corticosteroids and biologic therapies targeting IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 pathways.
source: GINA Asthma Guidelines 2021 — PMID 32979942 | Eur Respir J. 2020

text: Asthma management follows a 5-step treatment ladder. Steps 1–2 use low-dose inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) with as-needed SABA or as-needed ICS-formoterol. Steps 3–4 add long-acting beta-agonists (LABA) to ICS, with medium-high ICS doses. Step 5 uses high-dose ICS-LABA plus add-on biologics for severe eosinophilic asthma: mepolizumab and benralizumab target IL-5, dupilumab targets IL-4/IL-13, omalizumab targets IgE. Biologic therapy reduces exacerbations by 50–60% and achieves oral corticosteroid-sparing in severe asthma.
source: Pharmacotherapy of Asthma — PMID 30545200 | Lancet. 2019

text: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is characterised by persistent airflow limitation due to airway and/or alveolar abnormalities, caused primarily by significant exposure to noxious particles or gases, usually cigarette smoke. Diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 on spirometry. GOLD classification grades severity GOLD 1–4 by FEV1 % predicted. Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow disease progression, reducing FEV1 decline from 60 mL/year to 30 mL/year.
source: GOLD COPD Report 2021 — PMID 32877039 | Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2021

text: Long-acting bronchodilators (LABA and LAMA) are the cornerstone of COPD pharmacotherapy. Dual bronchodilation with LABA-LAMA combination (umeclidinium/vilanterol, tiotropium/olodaterol, glycopyrrolate/formoterol) reduces exacerbations and improves lung function more than monotherapy. Triple therapy (ICS-LABA-LAMA) is reserved for patients with frequent exacerbations and blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/µL. Roflumilast (PDE-4 inhibitor) reduces exacerbations in severe COPD with chronic bronchitis. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity, dyspnoea, and quality of life.
source: Pharmacological Management of COPD — PMID 31727409 | NEJM. 2019

text: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is the leading infectious cause of death worldwide. Streptococcus pneumoniae accounts for 30–40% of cases requiring hospitalisation. Severity assessment using validated tools — CURB-65 (confusion, urea, respiratory rate, blood pressure, age ≥65) or PSI/PORT score — guides site-of-care decisions. CURB-65 score 0–1: outpatient treatment; score 2: consider hospitalisation; score ≥3: hospitalise, consider ICU. Blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics in hospitalised patients.
source: Community-Acquired Pneumonia — PMID 30418471 | Lancet. 2018

text: Empirical antibiotic selection for community-acquired pneumonia follows site-of-care and severity. Outpatients without comorbidities: amoxicillin 1g TDS or doxycycline. Outpatients with comorbidities: amoxicillin-clavulanate plus macrolide, or respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin). Hospitalised non-ICU patients: beta-lactam plus macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy. ICU patients: beta-lactam plus either azithromycin or fluoroquinolone. Duration: 5 days for mild-moderate CAP if clinical stability achieved. COVID-19 pneumonia requires specific management pathway.
source: Antibiotic Therapy for CAP — PMID 33275120 | IDSA Guidelines. 2020

text: Sepsis is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection (Sepsis-3 definition). Organ dysfunction is identified by an acute increase in SOFA score ≥2 points. Septic shock is a subset with vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg and lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. In-hospital mortality for septic shock exceeds 40%. Early recognition using qSOFA (altered mentation, RR ≥22/min, SBP ≤100 mmHg) at the bedside facilitates timely intervention.
source: Sepsis-3 Definitions — PMID 32112386 | JAMA. 2016

text: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 Bundle recommends: measure lactate (re-measure if >2 mmol/L), obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L, and apply vasopressors for refractory hypotension targeting MAP ≥65 mmHg. Norepinephrine is the first-choice vasopressor. Vasopressin (0.03 U/min) is added second. Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day is reserved for refractory septic shock. Source control (drainage, debridement) must be achieved within 6–12 hours.
source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Bundle — PMID 30635113 | Intensive Care Med. 2019

text: Major depressive disorder (MDD) affects 280 million people worldwide and is the leading cause of disability. DSM-5 diagnosis requires ≥5 of 9 symptoms present for ≥2 weeks, including depressed mood or anhedonia: depressed mood, anhedonia, weight/appetite change, insomnia or hypersomnia, psychomotor agitation/retardation, fatigue, worthlessness/guilt, poor concentration, recurrent thoughts of death. PHQ-9 score ≥10 indicates moderate-severe depression warranting treatment. Suicide risk must be assessed at every visit.
source: Major Depressive Disorder — PMID 31617591 | Lancet. 2019

text: First-line pharmacotherapy for major depression includes SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine) and SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine). SSRIs are preferred for initial treatment due to tolerability and safety in overdose. Adequate antidepressant trial requires ≥4–6 weeks at therapeutic dose. Response rates are 50–60%; remission 30–40% with first agent. Non-response warrants dose increase, augmentation (lithium, atypical antipsychotics, buspirone), or switch. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is equally effective to antidepressants for mild-moderate depression and superior for relapse prevention.
source: Antidepressant Pharmacotherapy — PMID 30655025 | NEJM. 2019

text: Treatment-resistant depression (TRD) is defined as failure to respond to ≥2 adequate antidepressant trials. Esketamine (Spravato) nasal spray is FDA-approved for TRD and MDD with acute suicidal ideation, demonstrating rapid onset within hours. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) remains the most effective treatment for severe TRD, achieving remission in 60–80% of patients. Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) is a non-invasive alternative with 30–35% remission rates. Psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy with psilocybin shows promising results in early clinical trials.
source: Treatment-Resistant Depression — PMID 32741785 | NEJM. 2020

text: Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive, difficult-to-control worry about multiple domains lasting ≥6 months, accompanied by ≥3 of: restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance. Lifetime prevalence is 5–6%. GAD-7 score ≥10 indicates moderate severity. First-line treatments are SSRIs/SNRIs (escitalopram, venlafaxine, duloxetine) and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). Benzodiazepines should be avoided as first-line due to dependence risk; reserved for short-term acute management.
source: Generalised Anxiety Disorder — PMID 31588905 | Lancet. 2019

text: Ischaemic stroke constitutes 87% of all strokes and is caused by thrombotic or embolic occlusion of cerebral arteries. Rapid recognition using FAST (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency) enables timely reperfusion therapy. IV alteplase (tPA) remains standard of care within 4.5 hours of symptom onset in eligible patients, reducing disability by 30%. Mechanical thrombectomy is indicated for large vessel occlusion (LVO) within 6–24 hours and dramatically improves outcomes, with number needed to treat of 2.6 for functional independence.
source: Ischaemic Stroke Management — PMID 31492534 | Lancet. 2019

text: Secondary stroke prevention depends on stroke mechanism. Cardioembolic stroke (AF, valvular disease): long-term anticoagulation with DOAC. Non-cardioembolic ischaemic stroke/TIA: dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days then single antiplatelet long-term; high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg) targeting LDL <1.8 mmol/L; blood pressure control <130/80 mmHg. Carotid endarterectomy or stenting for symptomatic carotid stenosis ≥70% within 2 weeks of TIA/minor stroke reduces recurrent stroke risk by 50%.
source: Secondary Stroke Prevention — PMID 30129535 | NEJM. 2018

text: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common cause of dementia, accounting for 60–70% of cases globally, affecting 55 million people. Pathological hallmarks include amyloid-β plaques and neurofibrillary tau tangles. Diagnosis is increasingly biomarker-based: amyloid PET, tau PET, CSF Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio, and plasma phospho-tau 217 (p-tau217). Clinical staging: mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to AD → mild AD dementia → moderate → severe. MMSE and MoCA are validated screening tools. APOE ε4 allele is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset AD.
source: Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis and Management — PMID 32640687 | Lancet. 2020

text: Lecanemab and donanemab are anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies that significantly reduce amyloid burden and slow cognitive decline in early AD. Lecanemab reduced clinical decline by 27% versus placebo in the CLARITY AD trial and received FDA approval in 2023. Amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), including oedema (ARIA-E) and microhaemorrhages (ARIA-H), are important safety concerns requiring MRI monitoring. Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine provide modest symptomatic benefit across all stages but do not modify disease progression.
source: Disease-Modifying Therapy in Alzheimer's Disease — PMID 34129181 | NEJM. 2022

text: Breast cancer is the most common cancer in women globally, with 2.3 million new cases in 2020. Risk factors include BRCA1/2 mutations (lifetime risk 50–70%), age, dense breast tissue, early menarche, late menopause, nulliparity, HRT use, and alcohol. Screening mammography every 1–2 years from age 40–50 (guidelines vary) reduces breast cancer mortality by 15–20%. Tumour characterisation: hormone receptor (HR) status (ER, PR), HER2 status, and Ki-67 proliferation index guide treatment selection.
source: Breast Cancer Epidemiology and Screening — PMID 31851796 | Lancet. 2019

text: Treatment of early-stage breast cancer combines surgery (breast-conserving surgery + radiotherapy equivalent to mastectomy for stages I–II), systemic therapy based on molecular subtype. HR+/HER2- tumours: adjuvant endocrine therapy (tamoxifen 5–10 years for premenopausal; aromatase inhibitors for postmenopausal) ± CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib) for high-risk. HER2+ tumours: trastuzumab + pertuzumab-based chemotherapy. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC): neoadjuvant chemotherapy ± immunotherapy (pembrolizumab for PD-L1+, olaparib for BRCA-mutated).
source: Early Breast Cancer Treatment Guidelines — PMID 33207254 | NEJM. 2020

text: Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide, with 1.8 million deaths annually. Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) accounts for 85% of cases. Low-dose CT screening annually in high-risk individuals (age 50–80, ≥20 pack-year smoking history, current or quit within 15 years) reduces lung cancer mortality by 20–24%. Molecular profiling of advanced NSCLC for actionable mutations — EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, MET, RET, NTRK, KRAS G12C — is mandatory before initiating therapy as targeted therapies vastly outperform chemotherapy.
source: Lung Cancer Screening and Molecular Profiling — PMID 30280641 | NEJM. 2018

text: Immunotherapy has transformed advanced NSCLC management. Pembrolizumab monotherapy is first-line for PD-L1 ≥50% tumours without actionable mutations (OS 16 months vs 11 months for chemotherapy). Pembrolizumab + platinum-based chemotherapy benefits all PD-L1 expression levels. Nivolumab + ipilimumab improves OS regardless of PD-L1 status. Checkpoint inhibitor-related immune adverse events (irAEs) — pneumonitis, colitis, endocrinopathies, hepatitis — require prompt recognition and steroid management. Osimertinib is standard for EGFR-mutant NSCLC, achieving PFS of 18 months.
source: Immunotherapy in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer — PMID 33658772 | Lancet Oncol. 2021

text: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the third most common cancer and second leading cause of cancer death. Screening reduces CRC mortality by detecting precancerous adenomas and early-stage cancers. Colonoscopy every 10 years from age 45 (USPSTF 2021 recommendation) is the gold standard — allows direct visualisation, biopsy, and polypectomy. Alternatives: annual high-sensitivity guaiac FOBT or FIT, stool DNA test (Cologuard) every 1–3 years, CT colonography every 5 years. Patients with Lynch syndrome or FAP require surveillance from age 20–25.
source: Colorectal Cancer Screening Guidelines — PMID 31189536 | JAMA. 2019

text: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic systemic autoimmune disease characterised by symmetric polyarthritis, synovial inflammation, and progressive joint destruction. RF and anti-CCP antibodies are positive in 60–80% of cases (seropositive RA). Treat-to-target strategy aiming for DAS28 remission (<2.6) or low disease activity (<3.2) improves long-term outcomes. Methotrexate (MTX) 15–25 mg/week is the anchor DMARD for most patients. Hydroxychloroquine and sulfasalazine are added for triple DMARD therapy.
source: RA Treatment Guidelines — PMID 32659001 | Ann Rheum Dis. 2020

text: Biologic DMARDs are indicated for RA patients with inadequate response to conventional DMARDs. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab, golimumab, infliximab) are first-line biologics. IL-6 receptor inhibitors (tocilizumab, sarilumab) are alternatives, particularly when elevated CRP/ESR is a clinical concern. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib) are oral targeted synthetic DMARDs equally effective to biologics. All biologics and JAK inhibitors require screening for TB, hepatitis B, and vaccinations before initiation. Cardiovascular risk assessment is essential given accelerated atherosclerosis in RA.
source: Biologic Therapy in Rheumatoid Arthritis — PMID 30765264 | NEJM. 2019

text: Osteoporosis is defined by a bone mineral density (BMD) T-score ≤−2.5 at lumbar spine or femoral neck on DXA. FRAX tool calculates 10-year fracture probability incorporating BMD and clinical risk factors. Treatment thresholds: prior fragility fracture, T-score ≤−2.5, or FRAX major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%. Bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid) are first-line, reducing vertebral fractures by 60–70% and hip fractures by 40–50%. Calcium 1000–1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800–1000 IU/day are essential adjuncts.
source: Osteoporosis Diagnosis and Management — PMID 33602346 | Lancet. 2021

text: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is projected to cause 10 million deaths annually by 2050, surpassing cancer. ESKAPE pathogens (Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacter species) cause the majority of hospital-acquired infections resistant to standard antibiotics. MRSA (methicillin-resistant S. aureus) requires vancomycin or daptomycin. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) require ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, or cefiderocol. Antibiotic stewardship programmes reduce AMR rates, C. difficile infections, and hospital costs.
source: Antimicrobial Resistance — PMID 31150524 | Lancet. 2019

text: HIV infection affects 38 million people globally. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) suppresses viral replication to undetectable levels, restores immune function, and enables near-normal life expectancy. Universal ART initiation regardless of CD4 count is now standard of care. Preferred first-line regimen: bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine (Biktarvy) — a single-tablet once-daily regimen with high efficacy, barrier to resistance, and favourable safety profile. Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U): viral load <200 copies/mL eliminates sexual HIV transmission.
source: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy Guidelines — PMID 32220651 | Lancet. 2020

text: COVID-19 ranges from asymptomatic infection to critical illness. Risk factors for severe disease: age >65, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, chronic lung disease, immunosuppression. WHO clinical severity: mild (no hypoxia), moderate (SpO2 90–94%), severe (SpO2 <90% or RR >30), critical (ARDS, septic shock). Dexamethasone 6 mg/day for 10 days reduces 28-day mortality in patients requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation (RECOVERY trial, RR 0.83). Therapeutic anticoagulation benefits moderately ill hospitalised patients not requiring ICU-level care.
source: COVID-19 Clinical Management — PMID 33378609 | NEJM. 2020

text: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) reduces risk of hospitalisation or death by 89% in high-risk non-hospitalised adults with COVID-19 when initiated within 5 days of symptom onset. Remdesivir reduces hospitalisation by 87% in outpatients when started within 7 days. Molnupiravir reduces risk of hospitalisation/death by 30%. COVID-19 vaccines (mRNA platforms: BNT162b2, mRNA-1273) demonstrate 90–95% efficacy against severe disease and death. Hybrid immunity (vaccination + prior infection) provides broadest protection against variants. Long COVID affects 10–30% of those infected, causing fatigue, cognitive impairment, and dyspnoea persisting >12 weeks.
source: COVID-19 Antiviral Therapy — PMID 34706427 | NEJM. 2021

text: Hypothyroidism affects 5% of adults, more common in women and those >60 years. Primary hypothyroidism shows elevated TSH with low free T4. Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune) is the most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions, confirmed by elevated TPO antibodies. Levothyroxine is the treatment of choice, starting at 1.6 µg/kg/day (lower in elderly/cardiac patients). Target TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for most patients; 0.1–1.5 mIU/L during pregnancy. Dose adjustments based on TSH every 6–8 weeks until stable, then annually.
source: Hypothyroidism Management — PMID 31413600 | Lancet. 2019

text: Hyperthyroidism presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, tremor, and anxiety. Graves' disease (TSH receptor antibodies) causes 70–80% of cases. Treatment options: antithyroid drugs (methimazole/carbimazole, propylthiouracil) to achieve remission in 30–50% after 12–18 months; radioactive iodine (RAI) therapy most cost-effective; thyroidectomy for large goitre, suspected malignancy, or RAI contraindication. Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) control adrenergic symptoms immediately. Thyroid storm is a life-threatening emergency requiring ICU admission, high-dose antithyroid drugs, beta-blockade, steroids, and iodine solution.
source: Hyperthyroidism Treatment — PMID 30566890 | NEJM. 2019

text: Migraine affects 1 billion people worldwide and is the second leading cause of disability globally. Classified as episodic (<15 headache days/month) or chronic (≥15 days/month). Acute treatment: triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan) are first-line for moderate-severe attacks, providing pain freedom in 30–40% at 2 hours. CGRP receptor antagonists (gepants: ubrogepant, rimegepant) and 5-HT1F agonists (ditans: lasmiditan) are newer acute options without vasoconstrictive effects, suitable for patients with cardiovascular contraindications to triptans.
source: Migraine Acute Treatment — PMID 31504404 | Lancet Neurol. 2019

text: Migraine prevention is indicated for ≥4 headache days/month or significant disability. Traditional preventives: topiramate, valproate, beta-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol), amitriptyline, venlafaxine. Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) represent a major advance in preventive therapy, reducing monthly migraine days by 50% in 40–50% of patients with good tolerability. OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) injections every 12 weeks are approved for chronic migraine prevention (≥15 days/month).
source: Migraine Prevention Therapy — PMID 32342897 | NEJM. 2020

text: Chronic pain affects 20% of adults globally and is the leading cause of disability. Multimodal pain management combines pharmacological and non-pharmacological approaches. WHO analgesic ladder: Step 1 (mild pain): paracetamol and/or NSAIDs; Step 2 (moderate): weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) + non-opioid; Step 3 (severe): strong opioids (morphine, oxycodone, hydromorphone). Adjuvants at any step: antidepressants (amitriptyline, duloxetine) and anticonvulsants (gabapentin, pregabalin) for neuropathic pain components.
source: Chronic Pain Management — PMID 31326558 | Lancet. 2019

text: Opioid prescribing for chronic non-cancer pain has declined following recognition of risks: addiction, overdose, hyperalgesia, and endocrine dysfunction. CDC 2022 guidelines recommend: use lowest effective dose, avoid >90 MME/day, co-prescribe naloxone for high-risk patients, reassess benefits/risks every 1–4 weeks when initiating and regularly thereafter. Non-opioid alternatives: NSAIDs, duloxetine (neuropathic, musculoskeletal), pregabalin/gabapentin (neuropathic), topical agents (lidocaine, diclofenac), interventional approaches (nerve blocks, spinal cord stimulation), and psychological therapies (CBT, ACT).
source: Opioid Prescribing Guidelines — PMID 33002489 | CDC. 2022

text: Adult immunisation schedule (US CDC/ACIP 2024 recommendations): Annual influenza vaccine for all adults. COVID-19 updated vaccine annually. Tdap once then Td every 10 years. Pneumococcal: PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23 for adults ≥65 or high-risk. Shingles: Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine) 2-dose series for adults ≥50 — 90%+ efficacy, superior to live Zostavax. HPV vaccine through age 26 routinely, 27–45 by shared decision. MMR if no evidence of immunity. Hepatitis B 3-dose series for unvaccinated adults.
source: Adult Immunisation Schedule — PMID 31774989 | Ann Intern Med. 2024

text: Dietary patterns associated with reduced cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and all-cause mortality: Mediterranean diet (olive oil, fish, legumes, whole grains, nuts, moderate wine) reduces CVD events by 30% (PREDIMED trial). DASH diet reduces systolic BP by 8–11 mmHg. Plant-based diets reduce T2DM risk by 23%. Ultra-processed food consumption (>4 servings/day) is associated with 62% higher all-cause mortality. Dietary fibre 25–38 g/day reduces colorectal cancer risk by 10% per 10g increment. Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA + DHA 1–2 g/day) reduce triglycerides by 25–30%.
source: Dietary Patterns and Chronic Disease — PMID 31617577 | NEJM. 2019

text: Insomnia disorder affects 10–15% of adults chronically, with 30% experiencing occasional symptoms. DSM-5 criteria: difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months causing significant distress. Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is first-line treatment, superior to pharmacotherapy long-term, achieving remission in 80% of patients. Components: sleep restriction therapy, stimulus control, sleep hygiene education, relaxation training, cognitive restructuring. Digital CBT-I programs (Sleepio, Somryst) provide scalable access.
source: Insomnia Treatment Guidelines — PMID 30970188 | Lancet. 2019

text: Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) affects 1 billion people worldwide, defined by ≥5 obstructive respiratory events/hour (AHI). Symptoms: snoring, witnessed apnoeas, nocturnal gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth ≥10). Diagnosis by polysomnography or home sleep apnoea test. CPAP therapy is first-line, reducing AHI by >90% and improving daytime sleepiness, blood pressure (−2.5/−1.5 mmHg), and cardiovascular risk. Mandibular advancement devices are effective alternatives for mild-moderate OSA. Positional therapy for positional OSA (AHI worse supine). Weight loss of 10% reduces AHI by 26%.
source: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea Management — PMID 32160948 | Lancet. 2020

text: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD/GERD) affects 10–30% of Western populations. Typical symptoms: heartburn, regurgitation. Alarm features warranting urgent endoscopy: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, anaemia, age >55 with new-onset symptoms. Lifestyle modifications: weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoiding late meals, reducing trigger foods (fatty foods, caffeine, alcohol, citrus, chocolate). Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs: omeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole) are first-line pharmacotherapy, achieving symptom control in 80–90%. Long-term PPI use: risk of C. difficile, hypomagnesaemia, B12 deficiency — step-down to lowest effective dose.
source: GERD Management Guidelines — PMID 32427279 | Am J Gastroenterol. 2022

text: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) affects 11% of the global population and is the most common functional GI disorder. Rome IV criteria: recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week for ≥3 months, associated with ≥2 of: defecation, change in stool frequency, change in stool form. Subtypes: IBS-C (constipation), IBS-D (diarrhoea), IBS-M (mixed). Pathophysiology involves gut-brain axis dysregulation, visceral hypersensitivity, altered gut microbiome, and intestinal permeability. Low FODMAP diet reduces symptoms in 50–80% of IBS-D patients.
source: Irritable Bowel Syndrome — PMID 31474249 | Lancet. 2019

text: Low back pain (LBP) is the leading cause of disability worldwide, affecting 540 million people. 90% of acute LBP resolves within 6 weeks with conservative management. Red flags requiring urgent imaging: cauda equina syndrome (bilateral leg weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anaesthesia), fever with back pain, unexplained weight loss, progressive neurological deficit, age >50 with new onset, history of malignancy. Routine imaging in non-specific LBP is not recommended and can increase unnecessary procedures.
source: Low Back Pain — PMID 29573872 | Lancet. 2018

text: First-line management of non-specific low back pain: remain active and reassurance (avoid bed rest), paracetamol and/or NSAIDs for short-term pain relief, supervised exercise therapy (core strengthening, yoga, tai chi), and psychological therapies (CBT) for chronic LBP with psychosocial yellow flags. Spinal manipulation provides modest short-term benefit. Opioids should not be used as first-line; reserved only for severe acute LBP short-term. Epidural corticosteroid injections provide short-term relief for radicular pain but no long-term benefit. Spinal surgery benefits carefully selected patients with structural pathology.
source: Low Back Pain Management Guidelines — PMID 30064271 | JAMA. 2018

text: Obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) affects 650 million adults globally and is a major risk factor for T2DM, CVD, hypertension, OSA, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and multiple cancers. Aetiology: complex interaction of genetic predisposition, environment, psychology, and socioeconomic factors — not simply lack of willpower. Weight loss of 5–10% significantly improves metabolic comorbidities. Structured lifestyle interventions combining dietary modification, physical activity (150–300 min/week moderate intensity), and behavioural therapy achieve 5–8 kg weight loss at 12 months.
source: Obesity Management — PMID 32997947 | Lancet. 2021

text: Pharmacotherapy for obesity: semaglutide 2.4 mg/week (Wegovy) achieves 14.9% body weight reduction at 68 weeks in the STEP-1 trial — the most effective anti-obesity medication available. Tirzepatide (GLP-1/GIP dual agonist, Zepbound) achieves 20–22% weight loss at 72 weeks (SURMOUNT-1), approaching bariatric surgery outcomes. Older agents: orlistat (5% weight loss), naltrexone-bupropion, phentermine-topiramate. Bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass) achieves 25–35% weight loss and remission of T2DM in 60–80% of patients, with durable long-term outcomes.
source: Anti-Obesity Pharmacotherapy — PMID 34216463 | NEJM. 2021

text: Evidence-based cancer screening recommendations (USPSTF 2024): Colorectal cancer: colonoscopy every 10 years or FIT annually, ages 45–75. Breast cancer: mammography every 2 years, ages 40–74. Cervical cancer: Pap smear every 3 years or HPV test every 5 years, ages 21–65. Lung cancer: annual low-dose CT, ages 50–80, ≥20 pack-year history, current or quit within 15 years. Prostate cancer (PSA): shared decision-making ages 55–69. Skin cancer: no routine screening evidence in general population. Abdominal aortic aneurysm: one-time ultrasound for male smokers ages 65–75.
source: USPSTF Cancer Screening Recommendations — PMID 33961374 | JAMA. 2021

text: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells, causing absolute insulin deficiency. Onset typically before age 30 but can occur at any age. GAD65, IA-2, and ZnT8 autoantibodies confirm autoimmune aetiology. Management: multiple daily injections (MDI) using basal-bolus regimen (long-acting basal: glargine, detemir, degludec + rapid-acting bolus: aspart, lispro, glulisine) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/pump). Target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol (7%) balancing hypoglycaemia risk. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) significantly reduces HbA1c and hypoglycaemia.
source: Type 1 Diabetes Management — PMID 30291125 | Lancet. 2018

text: Closed-loop insulin delivery systems (artificial pancreas) automatically adjust insulin delivery based on continuous glucose monitoring data using control algorithms. The MiniMed 780G and Omnipod 5 systems achieve time-in-range (70–180 mg/dL) >70% in clinical trials, significantly reducing HbA1c and hypoglycaemia. Teplizumab, an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody, delays progression to clinical T1DM by 2–3 years in at-risk individuals with two or more autoantibodies and dysglycaemia — the first disease-modifying therapy approved for T1DM (FDA 2022).
source: Advances in Type 1 Diabetes Technology — PMID 34289308 | NEJM. 2021
